qa_pairs.question: What is the primary goal of understanding the various filesystems on SciNet?
qa_pairs.answer: Understanding the various filesystems, and how to use them properly, is critical to optimizing your workflow and being a good SciNet citizen.

qa_pairs.question: What type of high-performance filesystem is used on SciNet, with the exception of /archive?
qa_pairs.answer: The filesystems on SciNet, with the exception of /archive, are GPFS (IBM General Parallel File System).

qa_pairs.question: What are the advantages of GPFS for large datasets?
qa_pairs.answer: GPFS provides rapid reads and writes to large datasets in parallel from many nodes.

qa_pairs.question: Under what conditions does the GPFS filesystem perform poorly?
qa_pairs.answer: The filesystem performs quite poorly at accessing data sets which consist of many, small files.

qa_pairs.question: Why are small files considered wasteful of space on the scratch and project filesystems?
qa_pairs.answer: Such small files are also quite wasteful of space, as the blocksize for the scratch and project filesystems is 16MB.

qa_pairs.question: Is it an effective I/O solution for multi-process jobs to have each process write to its own file?
qa_pairs.answer: No, having each process write to a file of its own is not a scalable I/O solution because a directory gets locked by the first process accessing it, causing other processes to wait or the filesystem to time-out.

qa_pairs.question: What are two recommended strategies for managing I/O in multi-process jobs to improve scalability?
qa_pairs.answer: Consider using MPI-IO (part of the MPI-2 standard), which allows files to be opened simultaneously by different processes, or using a dedicated process for I/O to which all other processes send their data, and which subsequently writes this data to a single file.

qa_pairs.question: What is the primary purpose of the /home filesystem?
qa_pairs.answer: /home is intended primarily for individual user files, common software or small datasets used by others in the same group, provided it does not exceed individual quotas.

qa_pairs.question: What is the access status of the /home filesystem on compute nodes?
qa_pairs.answer: /home is read-only on the compute nodes.

qa_pairs.question: When should users consider /scratch or /project instead of /home?
qa_pairs.answer: Otherwise you may consider /scratch or /project if individual quotas on /home are exceeded.

qa_pairs.question: What is the main use case for the /scratch filesystem?
qa_pairs.answer: /scratch is to be used primarily for temporary or transient files, for all the results of your computations and simulations, or any material that can be easily recreated or reacquired.

qa_pairs.question: What type of files should be moved from /scratch for long-term storage?
qa_pairs.answer: Once you have your final results, those that you want to keep for the long term, you may migrate them to /project or /archive.

qa_pairs.question: Does the /scratch filesystem have backups?
qa_pairs.answer: No, /scratch has no backups.

qa_pairs.question: Is data on /scratch purged?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, /scratch is purged on a regular basis.

qa_pairs.question: What is the intended use of the /project filesystem?
qa_pairs.answer: /project is intended for common group software, large static datasets, or any material very costly to be reacquired or re-generated by the group.

qa_pairs.question: What is the expected characteristic of material stored on /project over time?
qa_pairs.answer: Material on /project is expected to remain relatively immutable over time.

qa_pairs.question: Why should temporary files be kept on /scratch instead of /project?
qa_pairs.answer: Temporary or transient files should be kept on scratch, not project, because high data turnover induces stress and unnecessary consumption tapes on the TSM backup system, long after this material has been deleted, due to backup retention policies and the extra versions kept of the same file.

qa_pairs.question: What happens if top directories in /project are renamed?
qa_pairs.answer: Even renaming top directories is enough to trick the system into assuming a completely new directory tree has been created, and the old one deleted, hence users should think carefully about naming conventions ahead of time.

qa_pairs.question: Who can access the /project filesystem on Trillium?
qa_pairs.answer: On Trillium /project is only available to groups with RAC allocation.

qa_pairs.question: What is the /bb filesystem, also known as the burst buffer?
qa_pairs.answer: /bb, the burst buffer, is a very fast, very high performance alternative to /scratch, made of solid-state drives (SSD).

qa_pairs.question: When should a user consider requesting the burst buffer?
qa_pairs.answer: You may request this resource if you anticipate a lot of IOPs (Input/Output Operations) or when you notice your job is not performing well running on scratch or project because of I/O (Input/Output) bottlenecks.

qa_pairs.question: What is the /archive filesystem?
qa_pairs.answer: /archive is a nearline storage pool.

qa_pairs.question: What is the primary function of /archive?
qa_pairs.answer: /archive is used if you want to temporarily offload semi-active material from any of the other filesystems.

qa_pairs.question: How long can material be kept on HPSS, which is the underlying system for /archive?
qa_pairs.answer: That material can remain on HPSS for a few months to a few years.

qa_pairs.question: Is /archive available to all users on Trillium?
qa_pairs.answer: No, on Trillium /archive is only available to groups with RAC allocation.

qa_pairs.question: What is /dev/shm on Trillium nodes?
qa_pairs.answer: /dev/shm is a ramdisk available on the Trillium nodes.

qa_pairs.question: How does the speed of ramdisk compare to real disk and Burst Buffer?
qa_pairs.answer: Ramdisk is much faster than real disk, and faster than Burst Buffer.

qa_pairs.question: What is the maximum amount of RAM that can be used as a temporary local filesystem via ramdisk on a Trillium node?
qa_pairs.answer: Up to 70 percent of the RAM on the node (i.e. 202GB) may be used as a temporary local filesystem.

qa_pairs.question: What is the $SLURM_TMPDIR environment variable on Trillium compute jobs set to?
qa_pairs.answer: On Trillium compute jobs, the environment variable $SLURM_TMPDIR will point to RAMdisk, not to local hard drives.

qa_pairs.question: What happens to the contents of $SLURM_TMPDIR after a job finishes?
qa_pairs.answer: The $SLURM_TMPDIR directory will be empty when your jobs starts and its content gets deleted after the job has finished.

qa_pairs.question: What is $BB_JOB_DIR?
qa_pairs.answer: $BB_JOB_DIR is a temporary directory on the burst buffer created by the scheduler for every job on Trillium.

qa_pairs.question: When is the $BB_JOB_DIR accessible, and from which nodes?
qa_pairs.answer: This directory is accessible from all nodes of a job.

qa_pairs.question: What is the intended use for $BB_JOB_DIR?
qa_pairs.answer: $BB_JOB_DIR is intended as a place for applications that generate many small temporary files or that create files that are accessed very frequently (i.e., high IOPS applications), but that do not fit in ramdisk.

qa_pairs.question: Which storage location is generally better for temporary files if they fit: ramdisk or burst buffer?
qa_pairs.answer: If the temporary files do fit in ramdisk, then that is generally a better location for them as both the bandwidth and iops of ramdisk far exceeds that of the burst buffer.

qa_pairs.question: How can users access ramdisk for temporary files?
qa_pairs.answer: To use ramdisk, you can either directly access /dev/shm or use the environment variable $SLURM_TMPDIR.

qa_pairs.question: Do Trillium compute nodes have local disks?
qa_pairs.answer: No, Trillium compute nodes have no local disks.
